sql_desc: Database penetration through vulnerable input fields.
xss_desc: Injecting malicious scripts into the client side.
sql_stats_text: Injections are consistently in the OWASP Top 10. Vulnerability occurs when user data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command.
sql_global_info: SQL injection allows an attacker to interfere with database queries.
hint_buy_confirm: Spend ${hint_price} from your balance for this hint? Are you sure?
surrender_confirm: Surrender? ${surrender_price} will be deducted from your balance.
low_balance_for_hint: ⚠ LOW_BALANCE: not enough balance for a hint (${hint_price}). Daily bonus +${daily_bonus} on next login.
flash_not_enough_for_hint: Insufficient funds for the hint. Wait for the daily bonus.
access_granted_text: Node successfully breached. System under control.
sql_theory_p1: > SQL injection alters part of a database query via user input when values are concatenated into SQL text instead of bound safely.
sql_theory_p2: > Classic bug: query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE login = '" + login + "'" — attacker-controlled SQL can be injected into login.
sql_theory_p3: > Auth bypass: a quote closes the string; OR 1=1 makes the predicate always true; -- or # comments out the rest of the original query.
sql_theory_p4: > UNION SELECT merges two result sets; column count/types must align — attackers often probe with ORDER BY n.
sql_theory_p5: > In MySQL, information_schema (tables, columns) reveals schema metadata without filesystem access.
sql_theory_p6: > Blind / time-based SQLi: no visible rows, but delays (SLEEP) or boolean differences still leak data.
sql_theory_p7: > Primary app-layer defense: parameterized queries (prepared statements) with bound parameters, not string interpolation.
sql_theory_p8: > OWASP lists this as A03: 2021 Injection; combine validation, least-privilege DB accounts, and query monitoring.
sql_theory_quiz_heading: [ QUIZ — 6 QUESTIONS. ALL ANSWERS MUST BE CORRECT ]
sql_th_q1: 1/6. What does adding OR 1=1 after a closed quote in a vulnerable WHERE clause do?
sql_th_q1o2: Makes the WHERE condition always true
sql_th_q2: 2/6. What best reduces SQLi risk in a typical web app?
sql_th_q2o3: Store passwords in plaintext for debugging
sql_th_q3: 3/6. Why are -- or # often used at the end of a payload?
sql_th_q3o1: To comment out the remainder of the original SQL
sql_th_q4: 4/6. What is required for UNION SELECT between two queries to work?
sql_th_q4o1: Same database name in both queries
sql_th_q4o3: Compatible column count (and types) in both SELECTs
sql_th_q6o1: Malicious input is stored and executed later in another query
theory_quiz_error: ACCESS_DENIED: all 6 answers must be correct.
xss_theory_p1: > XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is injecting executable code (usually JavaScript) into a page another user will see.
xss_theory_p2: > Reflected — payload bounces in the server response; Stored — saved and shown to many users; DOM-based — flaw in client-side JS parsing URL/data.
xss_theory_p3: > Without encoding, tags and attributes (script, img onerror, svg onload, javascript: in href) can execute in page context.
xss_theory_p4: > HttpOnly cookies are harder to steal via document.cookie but do not stop other XSS impacts (DOM changes, actions as the user).
xss_theory_p5: > Content-Security-Policy limits where scripts may load; combine with validation and contextual encoding.
xss_theory_p6: > Context matters: encoding differs for HTML, attributes, JS strings, and URLs.
xss_theory_quiz_heading: [ QUIZ — 6 QUESTIONS. ALL ANSWERS MUST BE CORRECT ]
xss_th_q1: 1/6. Where does malicious JavaScript run in a successful XSS attack?
xss_th_q2o1: Restricting where the browser may load and execute scripts
xss_th_q3o1: Is stored only in browser cookies
xss_th_q3o2: Appears in the server response without long-term server-side storage
xss_th_q3o3: Runs only in a cron job
xss_th_q4o3: Only if the image file exists on the server
xss_th_q5: 5/6. Is one generic encoding pass enough everywhere on the page?
xss_th_q5o1: Yes, one algorithm covers HTML, JS, and URLs
xss_th_q5o2: No — encode according to context (HTML, attribute, script, URL)
xss_th_q6o2: Blocks reading the cookie via document.cookie but not all XSS scenarios
csrf_desc: Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks that hijack authenticated sessions.
csrf_theory_p1: > CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) — attacker tricks user into performing unwanted action on another website where user is authenticated.
csrf_theory_p2: > Mechanism: User is logged into bank.com. Attacker sends link to malicious-site.com. Malicious site makes request to bank.com using user's browser.
csrf_theory_p3: > Browser automatically sends cookies (session token) with the request. Bank sees authenticated user and performs action (transfer money, change password).
csrf_theory_p4: > Vulnerable because the bank site cannot verify that the request came from legitimate user action on your site.
csrf_theory_p5: > Prevention: CSRF tokens (random, per-form/session), SameSite cookie attribute, Origin/Referer verification, POST only.
csrf_theory_p6: > SameSite cookie attribute prevents browser from sending cookie on cross-site requests; set to Strict or Lax.
csrf_lvl1_ex: Example: Browser loads the image and sends an authenticated request silently.
csrf_lvl2_ex: Hidden form auto-submits to accomplish more complex actions (POST data with multiple parameters).
csrf_lvl3_ex: GET parameter triggers via image load, video fallback, or automatic iframe refresh.
path_desc: Directory traversal attacks to access files outside intended directory.
path_theory_p1: > Path Traversal — attacker uses ../ sequences to navigate parent directories and access restricted files.
path_theory_p2: > Example: Application allows /downloads?file=readme.txt but vulnerable to: /downloads?file=../../../etc/passwd
path_theory_p3: > Exploits missing validation or incomplete filters. Simple blacklist for ../ can be bypassed with URL encoding or double encoding.
path_theory_p5: > Accessible files: /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /var/log/*, config files, .env, database backups, source code.
path_theory_p6: > Prevention: Whitelist allowed files, chroot/jail file access, use absolute paths, validate no ../ in input, disable directory listing.
path_lvl1_ex: Basic: Simply use ../ to go up directories until reaching root, then navigate to target (/etc/passwd).
path_lvl2_ex: When ../ is blocked, use encoding: %2e%2e%2f or ..%2f or variations like %252e instead of %2e.
path_lvl3_ex: Combine multiple bypass techniques: partial hex encoding %2e%2e, UTF-8 variants, multiple slashes etc.
auth_desc: Password guessing, token manipulation, and session hijacking attacks.
auth_theory_p1: > Authentication Bypass — circumventing login using weak passwords, token manipulation, or predictable session generation.
auth_theory_p2: > Category 1: Brute Force — attacker tries common passwords (password123, admin, letmein) until one works.
auth_theory_p3: > Category 2: JWT Manipulation — if server doesn't verify signature, attacker can modify token (change admin:false to admin:true) and re-encode.
auth_theory_p4: > Category 3: Session Prediction — if tokens are generated weakly (timestamp, user_id, random 4-digit), attacker can guess next token.
auth_theory_p5: > Prevention: Strong password requirements, rate limiting on login attempts, proper JWT verification with secret, use secure random generation.
auth_theory_p6: > Additional: Multi-factor authentication (2FA), account lockout after N attempts, HTTPS for transport, secure session management.
auth_lvl1_ex: Brute force typical weak passwords: admin/admin, admin/password, admin/123456
auth_lvl2_ex: JWT format: header.payload.signature. Modify payload: {"admin": true}. Encode in Base64. Server accepts if signature not verified.
auth_lvl3_ex: Token patterns: username_timestamp, MD5(user_id), incremental IDs. Guess based on known user data.
csrf_theory_quiz_heading: [ QUIZ — 6 QUESTIONS. ALL ANSWERS MUST BE CORRECT ]
csrf_th_q2: 2/6. How does a browser send cookies on a cross-site request?
csrf_th_q2o2: Automatically with each request if SameSite is not set
csrf_th_q2o3: Only on explicit request in code
csrf_th_q3: 3/6. Which cookie attribute protects against CSRF?
csrf_th_q4: 4/6. What is the main purpose of a CSRF token?
csrf_th_q4o2: Verify that a request comes from legitimate user action on your site
csrf_th_q5: 5/6. Can an attacker use CSRF through a simple tag?
csrf_th_q5o1: Yes, GET request executes automatically with cookies
csrf_th_q5o3: Only if the image exists on the server
csrf_th_q6: 6/6. Which HTTP header helps prevent CSRF attacks?
path_theory_quiz_heading: [ QUIZ — 6 QUESTIONS. ALL ANSWERS MUST BE CORRECT ]
path_th_q1: 1/6. What symbol is used to go up one directory level in Path Traversal?
path_th_q2: 2/6. Which file is commonly targeted in Path Traversal attacks on Linux?
path_th_q3: 3/6. How to bypass a filter that blocks "../"?
path_th_q3o3: There is no bypass, the filter is unbreakable
path_th_q4: 4/6. What does "chaining" mean in Path Traversal context?
path_th_q4o1: Multiple successive ../ to reach desired depth
path_th_q5: 5/6. Can UTF-8 encoding help bypass security filters?
path_th_q6: 6/6. What is the best prevention method against Path Traversal?
path_th_q6o2: Use a whitelist of allowed files
auth_theory_quiz_heading: [ QUIZ — 6 QUESTIONS. ALL ANSWERS MUST BE CORRECT ]
auth_th_q1: 1/6. What is a Brute Force attack?
auth_th_q1o1: Cracking passwords by guessing them one by one
auth_th_q1o2: Using SQL injections to gain access
auth_th_q1o3: Intercepting traffic between client and server
auth_th_q2: 2/6. What does JWT stand for in authentication context?
auth_th_q3: 3/6. How many parts does a JWT token consist of?
auth_th_q4: 4/6. What happens if server does not verify JWT signature?
auth_th_q4o3: Attacker can modify token (admin:true) and re-encode it
auth_th_q5: 5/6. How to predict the next session token?
auth_th_q5o3: Only if you know the user password
auth_th_q6: 6/6. What method is most effective against Brute Force attacks?
auth_th_q6o2: Rate limiting and account lockout after N attempts
